title: Financial Effects of Remote Product Delivery: Evidence from Hospitals
abstract: We study financial effects of remote product delivery in the healthcare industry. Exploiting staggered law adoption for identification, we find that telehealth provision redistributes hospital operations and access to capital away from rural communities. As urban telehealth providers acquire rural patients, rural hospitals experience decreased revenue and profit, credit rating downgrades, increased cost of capital, and ultimately risk of closure. Although telehealth reduces travel costs, some communities lose access to acute care. Overall, we conclude that remote healthcare services have financial consequences as well as real effects, and their benefits are unequally distributed.

article.1 A Theory of Telehealth Adoption:
Issue Section: Article Editor: Manju Puri Manju Puri Editor Search for other works by this author on: Oxford Academic Google Scholar

Reliance on advanced telecommunication technology gained new momentum during the COVID-19 pandemic. Recent studies have explored the impact of work-from-home labor supply on corporate performance (Barry et al. 2022;Duchin and Sosyura 2021) and commercial real estate markets (Gupta et al. 2022). This technology advancement further facilitates remote product delivery, potentially reshaping product market structure. This paper investigates the financial influences of remote business, with a focus on distributional effects in the healthcare industry.

We study remote product delivery by U.S. hospitals for two reasons. First, hospitals are essential to the healthcare system and the U.S. economy, accounting for nearly one-third of the $4.1 trillion in U.S. healthcare expenditures (CMS 2020) and offering economic benefits to their local communities. Second, healthcare provision via telemedicine provides an identification strategy allowing us to draw inferences with prepandemic data to mitigate confounding pandemic effects on hospitals. Telehealth provision grew rapidly before the pandemic, doubling almost every year after 2015.

By reducing travel costs, telehealth technology increases demand for healthcare as marginal customers benefit more, on net, from care. Besides growing patient headcounts, telehealth technology could increase provider profit by reducing costs associated with in-person visits and increasing patient turnover—provided insurers reimburse telehealth at the same rates as in-person care. However, our theoretical model suggests a more nuanced telehealth impact on the healthcare market structure. Extending theHotelling (1929)model, we introduce an endogenous telehealth adoption decision that reduces patient travel costs, leading to two competing forces that shape the equilibrium outcome. First, telehealth adopters benefit through the replacement effect of innovation (Arrow 1962), whereby telehealth services replace in-person services formerly provided by nonadopting competitors. Second, telehealth technology weakens the geographic protection of local hospitals by enabling patients to seek care from distant providers, resulting in heightened price competition among hospitals. The model implies that only high-quality hospitals benefit from adoption. Since high-quality hospitals concentrate in urban areas, we predict that (a) urban hospitals provide telehealth services while rural hospitals do not and (b) given telehealth provision, rural hospitals experience profit losses.

We then test these predictions empirically. Utilizing hospital financial statement data and detailed medical claim information, we provide evidence that telehealth adoption is primarily undertaken by urban hospitals, redistributing hospital revenues from rural providers. Rather than increasing patient headcount and profit evenly across providers, telehealth adoption results in rural providers losing patients to urban providers.

Establishing causal effects of telehealth adoption poses a challenge, given potential confounding factors affecting hospitals’ investment in new technology and variation in its utilization. Our identification strategy exploits a quasi-natural experiment: the staggered implementation of state-level telehealth parity laws requiring equivalent reimbursement of remote and in-person services by Medicaid programs and commercial insurers. Parity laws secure revenue streams from remote services, thus motivating their provision. Using medical claim data, we find that telehealth adoption redistributes revenue from rural to urban hospitals for in-person care and remote care. Telehealth consultation before in-person treatment (e.g., surgery) and remote monitoring after in-person treatment increase the likelihood of rural patients traveling to urban hospitals for in-person care. This redistribution of revenue is accompanied by a decrease in rural hospital profitability. Affected rural hospitals’ patient income over total assets, effectively the ROA of hospitals, decreases significantly by 2.4% relative to urban competitors.

After establishing effects on hospital operation, we examine the impact of telehealth parity laws on urban-versus-rural hospital access to capital markets. With secondary market trading data, we find that parity laws increase yields on affected rural hospital bonds by a significant 17 bps relative to bonds issued by urban hospitals in the same state. Estimates are consistent for credit-spread over maturity-matched after-tax Treasury rates (17.5 bps) and larger when we control for regional healthcare conditions (25.3 bps). The increase in yields following parity law adoption is similar in magnitude to the unconditional difference in rural and urban hospital bond yields (19.59 bps). Changes in yields and spreads are significantly greater for revenue bonds than for general obligation (GO) bonds, for bonds with preshock credit ratings below the sample median, and for hospitals without a supporting internal capital market from an affiliated healthcare system.

Given the heavy retail investor participation in municipal (hereafter “muni”) markets and the opacity of hospital financials, we examine the effect of parity laws on the credit ratings of bonds issued by affected hospitals. We find that credit rating agencies increase the average downgrade frequency for rural hospitals by 1.3% – 1.5% every month after the shock, comparable to the unconditional average downgrade propensity. Affected rural hospital bonds end up being rated an average of 0.9–2.0 notches lower as a result.

Because muni markets are illiquid and price discovery is therefore slow, we complement the secondary market analysis with primary market analysis to estimate a direct impact on new issuance costs, conditional on a hospital offering new bonds. Controlling for bond characteristics, we find that offer yields of bonds issued by affected rural hospitals increase by 20 bps relative to bonds issued by urban hospitals in the same state. This increase in offer yields translates to an additional $2 million in interest costs for the average affected rural hospital, estimated over the bond’s life followingGao, Lee, and Murphy (2019), which is economically substantial compared to the $1.59 million average annual rural hospital net income.

Classic corporate finance literature (e.g.,Modigliani and Miller 1958;Leland 1994) connects capital structure and default risk. With hospital leverage averaging 56%, these shocks to revenue, profit margin, and financing charges are likely to impact the viability of rural hospitals. Indeed, Cox proportional hazard models show that affected rural hospitals are significantly more likely to close following the adoption of parity laws.1These closures likely exacerbate inequalities in the availability of local healthcare services in rural versus urban areas. Our results and conclusions echo concerns raised byFuster et al. (2022)over unevenly distributed gains from better statistical technology in credit screening across socioeconomic groups. Also related are current studies documenting regional inequality in access to the financial sector as rural communities lose bank branch and mortgage market access (FRB 2019;Critchfield et al. 2019).

Our paper contributes to prior literature examining the effects of technology disruption on industrial organization. In the financial industry,Goldstein, Jiang, and Karolyi (2019)andKim (2021)document the impacts of Fintech on traditional banking. Other studies focus on the effects of Airbnb on local hotels (Zervas et al. 2017), Uber on labor markets (Fos et al. 2019), and Craigslist on local newspapers (Gurun and Butler 2012;Seamans and Zhu 2014;Gurun et al. 2016;Gao et al. 2020).

Also related is innovation theory (e.g.,Aghion and Howitt 2005) showing that firms innovate to escape competition. We contribute by showing that lagging firms may avoid technology development in fear of the consequent price war in the downstream product market. Differing from the traditional brick-and-mortar competition modeled inHotelling (1929), we assume that firms compete in two dimensions, price and innovation, and show that price competition becomes a binding constraint for technology adoption. We further provide evidence consistent with literature documenting the effects of industry concentration and competition on the cost of corporate bank loans (e.g.,Valta 2012) and stock returns (e.g., Hou and Robinson 1929). These papers examine market concentration and the cost of capitalacrossindustries, whereas we examine a shock reshaping product market structurewithinthe healthcare industry and its distributional effects across providers.

Our paper also contributes to literature examining the capital structure and investment decisions of hospitals. The tax code for nonprofit hospitals requires that hospital bond proceeds be directly invested, linking hospital investment decisions to external financing costs. Evidence shows hospitals increase capital expenditures with more endowment (Gentry 2002) and positive cash flow shocks (Adelino et al. 2015).2We contribute evidence that healthcare technology adoption has differential effects on the investment decisions and capital structure of urban and rural hospitals, leading to divestment of ICUs and human resources in rural hospitals. These adverse effects on rural hospital viability contribute to prior literature examining healthcare inequality (e.g.,Culyer and Wagstaff 1993; Weinhold and Gurtner 2012;Kaufman et al. 2016;Rajan et al. 2013). In particular,Zhou, Li, and Burtch (2023)complement our paper by examining the physician characteristics that make urban physicians willing to supply telehealth services and attractive to rural patients. They do not study the aggregated telehealth provision effects on healthcare facilities (hospitals) through capital market prices, investment policies, credit ratings and bankruptcy risks.

Finally, to the literature documenting determinants of public financing costs, we add evidence that healthcare technology advancement, designed to improve rural patient access to healthcare, increases rural hospital bond yields and credit spreads.3These adverse effects of regulatory changes on rural hospital cost of capital are comparable in magnitude to the advantageous effects of other regulatory changes (Medicaid expansion) on hospital bond yields documented byGao, Lee, and Murphy (2021).
This section presents testable predictions building on the work ofHotelling (1929). We extend the model by introducing an endogenous telehealth adoption decision that reduces travel costs for customers.

Hospitals make decisions in two stages. First, they simultaneously decide their telehealth coverage and travel coststj. Proofs and technical parameter restrictions are inInternet Appendix Section A.I.

Proposition 1. (i) In equilibrium, the urban hospital will fully utilize telehealth services, and the rural hospital will not provide any telehealth services, that is, t u = t _ and t r = t ¯ ⁠ . (ii) With telehealth adoption allowed, rural hospitals suffer profit losses compared to the case without any telehealth possibility.

Proposition 1highlights two competing forces through which telehealth services affect hospital profits. The first is the replacement effect of innovation (Arrow 1962), where telehealth services capture market share (previously in-person) from competitors. By reducing the travel cost to its location, a hospital can increase demand. However, the offsetting effect is that travel costs represent market differentiation so that locally each hospital can act as a monopolist. When travel costs reduce, customers consumption choice increases and hospitals engage in fiercer competition, leading to a price war as in the Bertrand model. Because of superior service quality, urban hospitals charge a higher price, generating the opposite telehealth adoption decisions. Urban hospitals prefer additional market shares since the marginal reward dominates a second-order price reduction effect. Rural hospitals optimally refrain from adopting telehealth services to avoid price wars, as the benefits of taking additional patients are lower. However, rural hospitals suffer two losses in their profits: a direct patient flow loss and an indirect price reduction due to a less differentiated market.
article.2 Institutional Details:
2.1 Telehealth parity law

We focus on the predominant telehealth modality, which involves using technology to connect patients with physicians located at distant sites for clinical care. A patient presents physically at a local healthcare facility, defined as the “originating site,” and a physician then joins the live video meeting from a distant location for patient examination and prescription.4The remote physician receives the service fee, and the originating site charges a facilitating fee, ranging from $50 to $350 for private insurers and $25 for Medicare.

The adoption of telehealth parity laws expanded this virtual market well before the COVID-19 pandemic.Internet Appendix Figure A.1exhibits the annual aggregate telehealth payments between 2012 and 2019 using Marketscan claim data, which represents 14% of the insured U.S. population. The telehealth market grew exponentially, with total annual revenue nearly doubling every year since 2015. Because the sample is representative (Aizcorbe et al. 2012), we estimate the totalreimbursedtelehealth payments in the United States is over $340 million ($48.22 million/14%) in 2019.

Besides technology innovation, telehealth reimbursement policy is the key facilitator of the growth seen inInternet Appendix Figure A.1. Without uniform regulation of reimbursement for private insurers or government programs, hospitals faced uncertain reimbursement for remote service.5This reimbursement risk disincentivized telehealth provision, prior to the adoption oftelehealth parity lawsin 36 states plus the District of Columbia.

We utilize state-level variation in telehealth parity law adoption as a quasi-random natural experiment to study the effects of telehealth provision. The treatment group consists of the 37 states (including DC) that adopt telehealth parity laws, as shown inInternet Appendix Figure A.2. California and Texas required reimbursement parity in the late 1990s. Most states (30 of 37) in the treatment group implemented between 2010 and 2020. This variation in timing enables us to investigate effects over a long sample period and reduces concerns that a particular macroeconomic shock confounds our results. InInternet Appendix Section A.II, we provide a case study for the legal details of parity laws using Washington, Minnesota, Oregon, Colorado, and New York as sample states. Parity laws vary in detail, though all 37 treated states mandate full reimbursement for live video treatment.

Fig. 2 Identification strategy Open in new tab Download slide

We support the predictions ofProposition 1with summary statistics.Internet Appendix Table A.1illustrates that telehealth adoption leads to the redistribution of revenues through two channels. First, rural patients switch from local in-person services to remote services offered by urban hospitals as predicted. As shown in panel A, 81.60% of rural patients (and nearly 100% of urban patients) choose urban telehealth providers; the majority of telehealth revenues are thus received by urban providers. Second, even for procedures that cannot be performed virtually, such as surgeries, telehealth adoption increases rural patient’ propensity of being served by an urban provider because telehealth services reduce the transaction costs of initial consultancy and recovery monitoring. In panel B, we calculate patients’ cumulative probability of receiving an in-person treatment from the same provider after their first telehealth meeting. We find that 36.6% of rural patients follow up by physically visiting their telehealth providers, with the majority of these in-person visits occurring within 180 days of the telehealth appointment. Telehealth services have apparent gateway effects increasing subsequent hospital admissions (Ayabakan et al. 2020). Through both channels, rural providers lose revenues due to virtual competition, and urban providers benefit.

2.2 Hospital bonds

Table 1, panel A, shows that 88% of sample hospital bonds are revenue bonds (12% GO bonds) compared to only 36% of nonhospital munis (64% GO bonds). A distinguishing feature of hospital revenue is its unpredictable nature, much of which is reimbursement from commercial insurers and government-backed insurance programs, such as Medicare. As a result, hospital revenues are sensitive to policy shocks in the healthcare market (Koijen et al. 2016), such as Medicaid expansions (Gao et al. 2021). Moreover, hospital competitions affect the bargaining process with insurers (e.g.,Gowrisankaran et al. 2015). These factors result in a relatively high default rate among hospital bonds compared to other municipal bonds (Gao et al. 2019), which is the key factor for muni yield spreads (Schwert 2017). It is therefore unsurprising to find inTable 1, panel A, that the average offer yield of hospital bonds is a significant 58 basis points (bps) higher compared to nonhospital munis.

Rural hospitals encounter additional challenges in accessing the muni market due to higher-than-average default risk compared to urban hospitals. Rural hospitals have lower patient volume due to low population density, so they lack economies of scale to cover fixed costs. Rural hospitals are also more likely to serve patients with Medicare and Medicaid, which are known for underpayment; hospitals receive an estimated 87 cents from every dollar spent by Medicare and Medicaid patients.6The underlying financial characteristics of rural versus urban hospitals are apparent fromTable 1, panel B. A typical urban hospital’s net income is over nine times larger than its rural counterpart. The difference in total revenue is more than $800 million, on average. Urban hospitals are on average five times larger than rural ones, measured by total assets, and admit almost 9,600 more patients annually. These features significantly constrain rural issuers’ external financing abilities. Panel C shows that the average rural bond yield in the secondary market is almost 20-bps higher, even though their average bond size is roughly half of their urban counterparts.

Nonprofit hospitals work through municipal entities to raise capital, and we classify rurality of the bond based on the municipal issuer’s location. For example, Bryan Health is a single-state nonprofit health system in Nebraska. Bryan Health issues municipal bonds through both Saline County (classified as rural) and Lancaster County (classified as urban) to fund projects in those respective counties. About 31% of the hospitals in our sample are system-affiliated, and their bonds account for 42% of the trade sample. We locate each bond into a specific county of issuance and assign rural and urban designations accordingly. We discuss implications of system affiliation for our empirical analyses in Section 5.
article.3 Data and Empirical Method:
3.1 Data

We collect hospital financial and operational data to examine whether revenue redistributes from rural to urban hospitals following telehealth parity laws. We also collect data on munis and county socioeconomic fundamentals to test whether the effects of revenue redistribution after telehealth parity laws impact hospital cost of capital. Our third data set is a proprietary database of medical claim information for privately insured individuals, providing direct evidence of telehealth adoption.

3.1.1 Hospital data

We construct a sample of Medicare-certified hospitals required to provide annual cost reports to the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS). These cost reports are analogous to annual reports for public corporations, disclosing financial variables at the hospital level.7They also disclose details on hospital operations, such as patients discharged, available beds, intensive care units (ICUs), and employees in all treatment units. Because disclosure requirements changed in 2009, we collect data from 2009 to 2018. We restrict our primary sample to 4,244 acute care hospitals and confirm that our results are robust to including other types of providers.8Cost reports are filed with CMS annually by individual hospitals, so our panel has 38,968 hospital-year observations.

Among these 4,244 sample hospitals, 1,987 operate in areas classified asRural. To measure county-level rurality, we collect Rural-Urban Commuting Area (RUCA) codes from the USDA, and classify ZIP code areas from the largest metro areas (1) to the most rural areas (10).9We classify an area asRuralif its RUCA code is greater than three and asUrbanotherwise. The cutoff follows the Census Bureau’s definition of an urbanized area, which means an urban nucleus of 50,000 or more people.10

3.1.2 Municipal bond data and county characteristics

We construct a U.S. muni bond sample from two sources: primary market issuance data and bond characteristics are gathered from the Mergent Municipal Fixed Income database (Mergent), and secondary market transaction data have been obtained from the Municipal Securities Rulemaking Board (MSRB). We limit the sample to bonds with a positive offering amount and coupon rate, and offered via conventional channels. We exclude issues by U.S. territories other than Puerto Rico. We identify 42,048 healthcare munis issued between 2000 and 2019 based on the indicated use of proceeds.11For these bonds, we collect secondary market transaction data from 2000 to 2019. We calculate secondary market yields at the bond-month level using the size-weighted average yield across all transactions for each bond in each month.

We estimate credit spreads in two ways: spread to maturity-matched Treasury bond and spread to maturity-matched yield on the tax-exempt Municipal Market Advisors AAA-rated curve (MMA curve) available from Bloomberg since 2001. To calculate the after-tax Treasury yield, we instrument marginal tax rates with estimates of top state rates by the NBER Taxism model available from 1977 to 2021.12We also use the credit rating history of each bond to identify ratings at the time of issuance for primary market analysis and at the time of transaction for secondary market analysis.13When rating is available from multiple rating agencies, we employ the harshest rating.

We geolocate each bond to a county using the first six digits of the bond’s CUSIP, which uniquely identifies the issuer. Using information from Bloomberg, we link issuers’ 6-digit CUSIPs to each county. We then match bond data to county-level characteristics using Federal Information Processing Standard (FIPS) codes for issuers. We collect county-level control variables over the 1999-2018 period from two sources. County population, employment, and income data are from the Bureau of Economic Analysis (BEA). Labor force statistics are from the Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS).

3.1.3 Medical claim and mortality data

We collect patient-level telehealth claim data using the IBM Marketscan database, which contains de-identified healthcare reimbursement information. The data provider sources claim information for employees, retirees, and dependents from over 260 medium-sized and large employers and 40 health plans. The database covers over 43 million privately insured individuals with employment-based health plans, representing roughly 14% of all insured and 20% of all privately insured U.S. population. InInternet Appendix Section A.III, we discuss in detail how we identify telehealth services and construct our claim-level sample.

We also license restricted-use mortality data from the CDC to construct the county-level annual mortality rate (per 1,000 residents) due to heart failure, chronic kidney disease, diabetes, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, or Alzheimer’s. The CDC data provide the precise cause of every death in every county in the United States, allowing us to accurately identify all deaths by location.

3.2 Identification strategy

Telehealth parity laws are adopted at the state level over several years (seeInternet Appendix Figure A.2). Treated states adopt parity laws and control states do not. Treated hospitals are located in treated states, and treated bonds are issued in treated states.Figure 2illustrates our identification strategy. State 1 represents treated states, and state 2 represents control states. Within each state, there is a rural-urban hospital/bond pair, A-B and C-D. The quasi-natural experiment is the adoption of the telehealth parity law by state 1. We predict that the difference in hospital income and profitability between hospitals A and B (treated hospitals) will increase relative to the difference between bonds C and D (control hospitals). Consequently, the difference in yields between bonds A and B (treated bonds) will increase relative to the difference in yields between bonds C and D (control bonds).

Following Proposition 1 , we first investigate whether the adoption of telehealth parity laws adversely affects the profitability of rural hospitals, thereby increasing their likelihood of financial distress. We estimate the following equation at the hospital level. β γ ε Y m , t = β Rura l m × Parit y j , t + γ ′ Control s m , t − 1 + Y r FEs + Hospital FEs + ε m , t . (1)

(2022) , we include a vector of 1-year-lagged county characteristics ( ⁠ Z k , t − 1 ⁠ ) including: (1) population level, (2) per capita income, (3) 1-year population growth, (4) 1-year employment growth, and (5) labor force participation. Including of State × Month FEs forces within-state comparison (A-B vs. C-D in the identification figure). This specification is close to a staggered triple-difference regression where an additional variable Parit y j , t is required. However, this variable is absorbed by the granular FEs in our specification.

TheState×MonthFEs absorb any unobserved state-level time-varying heterogeneity, and our identification comes from within-state differences between rural and urban hospitals. This identification thus is valid provided that the decision to enact a telehealth parity law is not dependent on the preadoption difference in yields for bonds issued by rural versus urban hospitals. We take several steps to verify this condition. First,Internet Appendix Table A.3shows that state-level hospital characteristics, population size, economic conditions, share of older people, and political preference do not predict the timing of parity law adoption under a Cox hazard model. The results alleviate the potential concern that urban hospitals strategically lobby for parity laws in states with vulnerable rural hospitals. Second, we search for anecdotal details to understand what prevents the fourteen control states from adopting parity laws and find no evidence suggesting legislators were concerned about financial distress for rural hospitals. Anecdotes suggest that the increased financial distress for rural hospitals after the parity laws is an unintended consequence that is not intentionally preempted by the control states.15Lastly, we validate parallel trends in rural and urban hospital financial conditions (Section 4.1), as well as those in rural versus urban hospital bond yields (Section 4.2) in various ways. In particular, results are robust to the interaction weighted (IW) estimator bySun and Abraham (2021)when using either the 14 nonparity states or the last-treated ones as the control units.

We test the capital market effects of telehealth parity laws by analyzing secondary market transactions for outstanding bonds issued by affected hospitals. For the treatment group, we limit our sample to bonds issued before the treated state adopts a parity law. We apply this restriction to address the concerns that (1) issuing hospitals endogenously change new bonds’ features after the shock and (2) a selected sample of issuers respond to the policy shock.16Since state law adoption is staggered, the analogous issue-date restriction for the control group is less obvious. We restrict our sample of control bonds to those issued in control states prior to 2014 for two reasons: (1) to avoid confounding effects from Medicaid expansion and (2) because most telehealth parity laws are implemented after 2015. We confirm that our results are not sensitive to this cutoff.17
article.4 Empirical Results:
4.1 Distributional effects of telehealth adoption

We investigate the distributional effects of telehealth adoption and report the results inTable 2. In columns 1 and 8, the outcome variable is hospital net income from patient services, defined as total patient revenues less contractual allowances and discounts, and total operating expenses. In column 1, the coefficient for the key variableRuralm×Parityj,tindicates that a typical rural hospital loses net patient income to its urban counterparts in treated states at a magnitude of 2.4% of total assets, equivalent to $1.5 million for an average affected rural hospital.18We also observe a positive coefficient ofParityj,twith a magnitude of 0.9% and at-stat of 1.48. In column 8, when replacingYearFE withState-YearFE, we obtain a consistent coefficient estimate of –2.7%, significant at the 1% level.

In columns 2 and 9, the outcome variable is net income, defined as total revenues less contractual allowances and discounts and total operating expenses, but without taking off other nonoperating expenses to avoid effects of increased financing costs. In column 2, the key variable coefficient indicates that treated rural hospitals experience a loss of 1.7% of total assets on hospital net income, relative to urban peers. The coefficient forParityj,tis significant at 5% level, indicating that urban treated hospitals see a profit increase equivalent to 0.9% of total assets, which translates to an average of $3.6 million.19Treatment effects on urban and rural hospitals move in opposite directions; urban hospitals benefit at the expense of rural hospitals. The sum of the coefficients ofRurali×Parityj,t(–1.7%) andParityj,t(0.9%) indicate anetloss to affected rural hospitals of 0.8% of total assets. In column 9 withState-YearFE, the net income loss to affected rural hospitals is 1.6% of total assets, significant at 5%.

In columns 3 and 10, treated rural hospital profit margin significantly decreases by 0.8%, suggesting that the marginal lost patient was associated with higher-than-average profitability for the hospital. In column 4, annual patient admissions per bed decreases significantly by a magnitude of 2.3 for treated rural hospitals and increases by 1.6 for urban treated hospitals. To illustrate the economic impacts of these changes, we note that a typical rural hospital has around $44.57 million revenues from inpatient services. The CMS cost report does not provide a separate breakdown of the profit margin for inpatient services. Based on the March 2023 Medicare Payment Advisory Commission Congress reports,20the average rural inpatient margin ranged from 3.9% to 5.2% between 2017 and 2019, indicating that the average inpatient net income falls between $1.74 million and $2.32 million. After the shock, revenues are expected to decline by 7.35%, estimated by the coefficient of 2.3 over the average of 31.31 rural admissions per bed. Moreover, profit margins will decrease to a range of 3.1% to 4.4%. Altogether, a ballpark estimate of the profit losses is between $0.46 and $0.50 million.21It is worth noting that these magnitudes are smaller than the $1.5 million losses implied by column 1. This is because we do not account for profit losses from outpatient services, and assume that hospitals do not reduce the number of available beds.

These results align withProposition 1: following the adoption of telehealth parity laws, urban hospitals leverage telehealth services, gaining market share and profit, while rural hospitals experience a decrease in patient flow and a reduction in prices, resulting in a loss of net income.22

We next examine the effects on hospital net wealth and wealth growth using hospital fund balance. Key variable coefficients indicate a significant 3% to 4.1% decrease in hospital fund balance to total assets ratio (columns 5 and 12) and a significant 1.6% to 1.7% decrease in hospital fund balance growth to total assets ratio (columns 6 and 13) among treated rural hospitals relative to urban peers. In columns 7 and 14, we observe a significant 2.9% – 3.9% increase in leverage, equivalent in magnitude to 5.7% – 7.6% of the unconditional average leverage among rural hospitals. Lower net wealth and higher debt amounts endogenously increase rural hospitals’ propensity for bankruptcy (e.g.,Leland 1994), consistent with hospital closure results inTable 7. To support the causal inference that telehealth adoption leads to the deterioration in rural hospital operations and financial conditions documented inTable 2, we next explore the dynamic effects in a 10-year window around telehealth adoption and plot the parallel trend figures inFigure 3to confirm that there exist no significant changes leading up to the shock.

In addition, we provide evidence that this policy change redirects patient flow from rural to urban hospitals in the treated state, rather than increasing either the customer base or the frequency of more expensive services for the healthcare industry as a whole. InInternet Appendix Table A.4, panel A, we estimate the state-wide effects of telehealth adoption; treated states as a whole experience no significant change in average hospital income, profitability, or aggregate patient and service flow. In panel B, we document similarly insignificant results for the average treatment effects across all individual hospitals.

In Section 1, we argue that telehealth adoption redistributes revenues from rural to urban hospitals in two ways: telehealth visits and in-person follow-up visits. We present supporting evidence inInternet Appendix Table A.5by aggregating the claim information from Marketscan to provider-year-level observations. In this table, the coefficients ofParityp,tmeasure revenue growth in different categories for urban telehealth providers after their states pass parity laws. Column 1 examines direct benefit from telehealth visits; the coefficient estimate suggests significant growth of 44% for urban hospitals. This result is consistent with rural patients switching from local in-person services to remote services offered by urban hospitals. For treatments that cannot be performed virtually, affected rural patients more likely choose urban providers if telehealth provides access for consultancy and recovery. We proxy this channel with revenues generated by follow-up visits in column 2 and find that urban telehealth adopters experience a 28% revenue growth in this category. In column 3 we find consistent magnitudes when we combine the revenues from both categories.

The large negative coefficients of the interaction term indicate that rural providers lose in the telehealth market, as they face entrants from urban areas. The results imply that revenue losses for rural hospitals are attributed to both virtual and in-person services. The magnitudes of the coefficients are large, consistent with the fast-growing overall market size inInternet Appendix Figure A.1.

4.2 Bond yield in the secondary market

After documenting the distributional effects of telehealth adoption on hospitals, we next test the effects on outstanding hospital bonds’ secondary market pricing. We estimateEquation (2)withYieldi,tas the dependent variable and report the results inTable 3. The coefficient for the key explanatory variableRurali×Parityj,treported in column 1 is 17 bps, indicating a significant increase in the urban-rural yield difference in treated states compared to bonds issued by hospitals in control states. This increase is almost equivalent to the unconditional difference in the cost of capital (19.59 bps) for rural providers (Table 1, panel B). These basis points translate to approximately $1.55 million in interest expense for a typical affected rural hospital over the life of the bond.23The economic magnitude is large, given the average annual net income for rural hospitals is approximately $1.59 million. The coefficient estimate ofRuraliis insignificant across specifications, indicating that our model sufficiently controls for the differences between urban and rural hospital bonds unrelated to the treatment.

Because of geographical differences in U.S. healthcare markets, variations in local market concentration may affect the findings in column 1. We thus add fixed effects to the specification in column 2 generated by the interaction between Hospital Referral Regions (HRRs) and year–month.24We obtain stronger results (25.16 bps) in column 2 after controlling for this variation.

Columns 3 and 4 ofTable 3show that results are not sensitive to calendar-based control group restrictions discussed in Section 3.2. By dropping control states without parity laws, we essentially estimate a simple DiD model in column 4 where rural hospital bonds in treated states are “treated” and urban hospital bonds issued in the same state serve as the control group. Given the robustness of these results, we utilize bonds issued in control states prior to 2014 for our empirical analysis unless specified otherwise.

The increase in the difference between urban and rural hospital bond yields could obtain because rural hospital bonds in treated states have higher yields relative to urban hospital bonds issued in control states or because urban bonds generally have lower yields. In column 5, we replace theState-MonthFEs withIssueFE andMonthFE to obtain the coefficient ofParityj,t⁠.25This insignificant coefficient has a small magnitude (0.94 bps), suggesting that yields of urban hospital bonds issued in treated states are not affected. Given that munis carry low credit risk relative to other assets classes and offer limited upside potential, yield changes are more likely driven by perceived default risk. Thus, the marginal increase in profits and revenues are less likely to be reflected in the cost of capital for urban hospital issuers with highex antecredit quality. The coefficient of the interaction term is significantly positive with a coefficient estimate of 11.45 bps, and the overall effect on treated rural hospital bonds remains highly positive. Therefore, the within-state inequality in the hospital cost of capital is primarily a result of distress in rural hospitals.

Columns 6–9 ofTable 3replicate previous results using alternative measures of bond prices:Spread(to maturity matched after-tax Treasury) andSpreadMMA(to maturity matched yield on the MMA curve). Results are robust and conclusions remain unchanged.

We conclude that the effects of telehealth parity laws take roughly 1 to 2 years to significantly emerge and persist forward. Several explanations exist for the delayed reaction. The illiquidity of the muni market dampens price discovery. (We, therefore, examine primary market effects in the next section.) Additionally, the adoption of telehealth parity laws spans a long horizon. We thus split the treatment groups into early-adopting (before Jan 2015) and late-adopting (after Jan 2015) states and present the dynamic effects inInternet Appendix Figure A.4. Treatment effects in late-adopting states are immediate and significant, suggesting that investors learn to price these effects. To further support the parallel trend assumption, we visualize the treatment effects of parity laws on urban and rural regions separately inInternet Appendix Figure A.5, where we estimate and plot the coefficients ofParityj,tnin the urban and rural treatment groups. This figure confirms the result in column 5 ofTable 3that hospital cost of capital stays unchanged in urban areas and drifts upward in rural ones.

We assess the impact on the credit ratings of bonds issued by affected rural hospitals inTable 5. Ratings are especially important in muni markets due to heavy retail investor participation and the opacity of hospital financials. We employEquation (2)with two modifications. The dependent variables measure hospital bond credit conditions:Ratingi,tis the numeric value of bondi’s credit rating in monthtwith the potential range of 0 (D) to 21 (AAA). In columns 1 to 3 ofTable 5, outstanding bonds issued by affected rural hospitals are more likely to be downgraded, leading ratings 0.9–2.0 notches lower. This magnitude of rating change is large compared to prior analysis of credit rating changes in the muni market (e.g.,Cornaggia et al. 2020). Downgrade frequency increases significantly by 1.3% – 1.5% for treated rural providers in columns 4 to 6. The economic magnitude is comparable toDowngradei,t’s unconditional average.

4.3 Robustness check of the secondary market results

We interpret the preceding evidence indicating that telehealth services redistribute revenues from rural to urban hospitals within treated states, resulting in a higher cost of capital for rural hospitals. An alternative explanation is that the concurrent rural population outflows in states with parity laws exceed the rural-to-urban population shift in control states.28If so, the diminished local tax base in rural areas should also impact the yields for nonhospital municipal bonds. InInternet Appendix Table A.7, panel A replicates the tests inTable 3for the nonhospital bond sample described in panel A ofTable 1. We observe insignificant coefficients for the interaction variable, despite a sample size of over 8 million observations. Also, theR2is nearly zero for this placebo test, in contrast to theR2ranging from.50 to.65 in our baseline regressions presented inTable 3. Insignificant results in the placebo test support our interpretation of results inTable 3.

To alleviate the potential concern that states adopt parity laws at times when credit risk is about to increase, particularly for lower-quality borrowers, we run an alternative placebo test in which the treatment group consists of higher-yield (above sample median) nonhospital munis and the control group consists of lower-yield nonhospital munis. These results inInternet Appendix Table A.7, panel B, are insignificant across all three outcomes, suggesting that the differential post-adoption urban-versus-rural effects are only in hospital bonds.

Another placebo test randomly selects 37 states into a pseudo treatment group and randomly assigns pseudo event dates between January 2010 and December 2018. We replicate the tests fromTable 3with these placebo shocks, and report the results inInternet Appendix Table A.8. These insignificant results suggest that the urban-rural differences in hospital cost of capital are not affected by random shocks in the same sample period.

We exploit county-level data on broadband internet access, a necessary condition for the utilization of telehealth service, to strengthen our identification. Rural hospitals in counties with limited broadband access should be less affected by telehealth parity adoption. Thus, we do not expect the cost of capital to change for such hospitals after their states pass parity laws. Internet providers are required to file Form 477 to the FCC by listing the locations where they offer internet services at certain speeds.29The FCC then reports the percentage of population without high-speed providers. We sort rural and urban counties into tertiles based on broadband access in 2016.30In the rural tertile with the worst internet coverage, more than 45.5% of residents have no broadband access (panel A ofInternet Appendix Table A.11). Parity law adoption does not affect rural hospital bond yields in this tertile (panel B, column 1, reports an insignificant coefficient for the key variableRurali×Parityj,t⁠). In the medium rural tertile, the coefficient for the key variable indicates an effect of 22.57 bps on rural hospital bond yields, with at-statistic of 1.54. We obtain the strongest effects in the rural tertile with best broadband access, where only 9.7% of residents have no broadband access. Results from this cross-sectional analysis validate our interpretation of results in Section 4.2.

InInternet Appendix Table A.12, we replace the credit rating at the time of transaction, a control variable for secondary yields analysis inEquation (2), with either initial rating or lagged rating in the previous trading month.31This robustness test alleviates the potential concern that the rating at transaction already reflects the deteriorated financial condition of affected rural hospitals and thus confounds our estimation of the effect on yields. Finally, we acknowledge that the coefficients ofRuralinTable 3are close to zero, which seems inconsistent with riskier rural issuers. These insignificant differences are driven by the granular rating controls that absorb broad default risks in the rural area. Once we drop rating measures in the controls, coefficients forRuralbecome positively significant in panel C. Across all panels inInternet Appendix Table A.12, our estimates of parity law impacts remain consistent and significant.

4.4 Bond issuance in the primary market

We examine the impact of telehealth parity laws on hospital bond yields in primary markets to address potential concerns in secondary market analyses. Municipal bonds are thinly traded, and secondary market trading may have incomplete price discovery. Also, dollar estimates of increased interest expenses in Section 4.2 are hypothetical because the coupons on outstanding bonds are fixed. Primary market analysis offers estimated real effects of telehealth parity laws on hospital cost of capital in basis points and dollar terms. Secondary market analysis is equally important since the primary market analysis is subject to selection issues: the composition of new issuers may endogenously change after the telehealth parity law is in place. If adversely affected rural hospitals refrain from issuing bonds in the post-shock period due to the negative impact on their borrowing costs, then the effects are unobservable. The results in this section offer a direct estimate of the new issuance cost, conditional on a hospital’s willingness to offer bonds given the prices they face.

We estimateEquation (2)with offer yields and spreads as dependent variables and report the results inTable 6. To be consistent with our secondary market analysis, we employ the same specification and require that parity states adopt parity laws since 2000. The difference in issuance costs between urban and rural hospital issuers, captured by the interaction term, increases by 16 to 21 bps after the shock. Column 1 implies a 19.59-bps increase in offer yield, translating into approximately $2 million in new issuance cost. The magnitude of this effect aligns with what is documented in the secondary market.

Given the concern over potential selection effects, we test whether telehealth parity law adoption affects issue frequently differently. For this test, we create a panel of yearly data at the county level.32We estimateEquation (2)with a new dependent variableIssuek,tthat takes the value of one if at least one hospital bond is issued in countykin yeart, and zero otherwise. We include lagged county characteristics (vectorZk,t−1inEquation (2)) as control variables along withCountyFEs. Results inInternet Appendix Table A.13suggest that hospitals in urban counties reduce new issuance frequency by a significant 3% following the state adoption of a telehealth parity law. However, the urban-rural difference in issuance frequency widens by 3.8%–4.4%, approximately 40% of the unconditional average probability (10.6%) of hospital bond issuance in rural areas. The net effect implies that hospitals in rural counties issue more frequently even though the shock increases their borrowing costs. We interpret these results as a substitution effect between internal cash flow generation and external financing, consistent with the pecking order ofMyers and Majluf (1984): urban hospitals generate higher internal cash flows and reduce new debt issuance; rural hospitals do the opposite.

In column 4 ofInternet Appendix Table A.13we test whether telehealth parity adoption affects the amount of capital raised, conditional on a new bond issuance. Because hospitals are required to fully invest the proceeds of tax-exempt bond issues, we predict that this dollar measure of issuance size should be insensitive to the shock, conditional on issuance.33This is what we find.

Overall, the primary market results suggest that rural hospitals increasingly depend on external financing after the adoption of telehealth parity, despite facing elevated borrowing costs. The secondary market tests are useful for gauging price impact on current investors. The primary market tests assess the real effects on affected municipal hospitals. Together, these analyses lead us to conclude that telehealth parity laws adversely affect rural hospitals’ cost of capital relative to urban hospitals operating in the same state.

4.5 Effects on hospital closures

Next, we consider the real effects of financial distress induced by telehealth adoption for rural hospital investment and viability. Given the effects on affected rural hospitals’ borrowing costs and net income, we predict that rural hospitals in treated states are more likely than other hospitals to close. We identify 129 hospital closures from 2010 to 2020 from our sample and estimate a Cox proportional hazards model with hospital closure as the outcome variable.34The results are reported inTable 7. Columns 1 and 2 indicate that affected rural hospitals are indeed at a higher risk of closure. In the other columns, we split the sample by rurality and find that parity law adoption significantly increases the propensity of closure only in the rural areas. To quantify the economic magnitude, we plot the estimated effects ofRuralm×Parityj,ton hospital survival probability over time inFigure 5. Over a 9-year horizon, an affected rural hospital is approximately 4% more likely to close compared to others. This difference is over the 3% cross-sectional closure likelihood (129 of 4,281). Because the closure probabilities of rural hospitals in nonparity states are almost the same as those of urban hospitals, we conclude that the “Rural & Parity” results inFigure 5are driven by the telehealth parity laws rather than worsening conditions in rural areas. We test the proportional hazards assumptions using Schoenfeld Residuals for the key variablesParity×RuralandParity⁠. Theχχ2s range from 0.01 to 0.67, andp-values are between.41 and.94, indicating that we cannot reject the assumption in all specifications.

In a corporate finance setting, employee downsizing and asset divestiture is a common response to financial distress (e.g.,Denis and Denis 1995;Kang and Shivdasani 1997;Benmelech et al. 2021;Falato and Liang 2016). Even if affected rural hospitals are not bankrupt, they can divest physical assets or human capital. This is what we find inInternet Appendix Table A.14; the number of ICUs, number of employees, and average physician salaries all significantly decrease among affected rural hospitals after parity law adoption.

Our results suggest that the consequences of telehealth adoption include indirect financial effects as well as direct healthcare effects. The benefit for telehealth users includes access to improved quality of care and reduced travel costs (e.g.,Dorsey 2013;Ayabakan et al. 2020). We hypothesize that divestment and closure of healthcare facilities in affected rural areas generate indirect externality tonontelehealthusers. To isolate indirect welfare costs, we present supporting evidence from medical services that typically require physical presence, such as emergency visits. The CMS ER-performance measure indicates that patients in affected rural areas experience longer wait times for admission to inpatient services and significant delays in receiving pain management (seeInternet Appendix Table A.15). We discuss welfare and policy in greater detail inInternet Appendix Section A.IV.
article.5 System Affiliation:
Lastly, we consider the increasing prevalence of health systems, many of which span urban and rural counties and operate across multiple states. These systems essentially provide internal capital markets affecting hospital operations and financial risk. For example,Lewis and Pflum (2015)argue that hospital systems affect bargaining power for reimbursement rates. In our sample, 40% of rural hospitals belong to multihospital systems that operate across counties or states and include urban branches.

We first revisit the question of system affiliation effects to our theoretical foundation. Proposition 2 inInternet Appendix Section A.Isuggests that the impact of telehealth adoption on hospital viability is likely mitigated for system-affiliated hospitals. If the integrated system operates an internal capital market and distributes profits across its branches, then rural hospitals receive subsidies from the system, reducing the risk of default. Consequently, muni yields do not necessarily increase, even if the rural hospital itself has lower profits.

To explore the role of system affiliation, we identify a subsample of affiliated hospitals and conduct analysis on the effects of telehealth adoption at both hospital and bond levels. We identify whether a hospital is part of a chain from lines 141-143 of hospital cost reports. We match these organization names with our muni bond sample using the project description. We replicate baseline empirical analysis fromTables 2and3separately for system-affiliated and independent hospitals and report the results inTable 8. In panel A, the outcome variables are net income, profit, and net wealth, scaled by total assets. Effects on profitability and net wealth are statistically significant in the subsample of independent rural hospitals, but insignificant among system-affiliated ones. The profit increase following telehealth parity laws are concentrated in urban hospitals affiliated with systems, indicated by the coefficient ofParityj,t⁠. In panel B, the effects of telehealth parity laws on bond spreads are concentrated in independent rural hospitals, with a significant (5% level) coefficient estimate of 21 bps for the yield outcome (compared to 17 bps in the full sample analysis) and significant at 5% level. Effects on system-affiliated rural hospitals are insignificant.

Finally, we examine the probability of a rural hospital being absorbed into a system and report the results inInternet Appendix Table A.16. We hypothesize that the telehealth shock may serve as a catalyst for some rural hospitals to be absorbed into health systems to survive. The outcome variable isTargetm,twhich takes the value of one if a hospitalmis absorbed into a system in yeart, and zero otherwise. Among independent hospital acquisitions, the probability of a rural hospital absorbed into a system significantly increases by 2.4% to 3.2% after telehealth parity laws. Results are robust among deals where system-affiliated hospitals are absorbed into another system, but the magnitude and significance are diminished compared to the case of independent hospitals. The probability of urban hospitals becoming targets significantly decreases following parity law adoption, as expected given the benefits to these hospitals shown inTables 2and3.

Overall, we conclude that affiliating with a larger system eases the financial burdens on rural hospitals. These results complement prior literature documenting benefits of internal capital markets in other settings (e.g.,Matvos and Seru 2014;Larrain et al. 2019;Santioni et al. 2020;Kabbach-de-Castro et al. 2022). However, it is important to approach policy implications with caution. Consolidation resulting from acquisitions in the healthcare industry has been linked to higher service costs and diminished quality (e.g.,Gaynor and Town 2011). Our results cannot assess aggregate welfare effects.
article.6 Conclusion:
We study remote product delivery of healthcare provision, focusing on the distributional effects and financial consequences for rural versus urban hospitals. We first document that telehealth provision from urban hospitals siphons patient revenue from affected rural hospitals, resulting in lower net income and profit margin and higher leverage. We then establish that these effects on operations result in credit rating downgrades for affected rural hospital bonds and are priced in both the secondary and primary hospital bond markets. DiD results indicate that affected rural hospitals face a 20-bps increase in offer yields following the entrance of urban hospital competition, which translates to an additional $2 million in financing charges over the life of the bond for the average affected rural hospital. This effect is economically large relative to the $1.59 million average rural hospital net income.

Increased cost of capital results in negative real effects for rural hospitals on investment in human capital (physicians and nurses) and Intensive Care Units. Cox proportional hazard models show that affected rural hospitals are ultimately more likely to permanently close, resulting in a loss of acute care to affected communities. We discuss welfare effects and suggest measured policy prescriptions in detail inInternet Appendix Section A.IV. Although rural patients voluntarily select remote care from urban hospitals, our results indicate an unintended loss of acute care in communities with financially distressed hospitals.
